dating.display: oldest prose Upaniṣads (Bṛhadāraṇyaka, Chāndogya) c. 700–500 BCE; the classical set composed over centuries
dating.basis: Relative chronology by language and cross-reference (Olivelle's assessment); absolute dates are inferential, as with all Vedic texts.

summary: The speculative culmination of the Vedic corpus: dialogues — often staged as debates, with kings, women (Gārgī, Maitreyī), and rival teachers — that turn ritual questions into questions about consciousness, death, and the self. Core theses: ātman = brahman; karma and rebirth get their first clear articulations here.
whyItMatters: Every subsequent Indian philosophical system positions itself relative to these texts. Their modern global reach is documented, not vague: a Persian translation (Dārā Shikōh, 1657) reached Europe as Anquetil-Duperron's Latin Oupnek'hat (1801–02), which Schopenhauer famously called 'the solace of my life' — a traceable transmission chain from Sanskrit to European philosophy.

throughLines.to: Global philosophy of consciousness (documented reception chain)
chain: Schopenhauer's explicit engagement (Parerga and Paralipomena; 'the solace of my life'), then 19th–20th c. philosophy and comparative religion

sources.label: App, U., Schopenhauer's Compass (2014) — the documented reception
Olivelle, P., The Early Upaniṣads: Annotated Text and Translation (OUP, 1998)
Olivelle, P., The Early Upaniṣads (OUP, 1998)
App, U., Schopenhauer's Compass (2014)
Brereton, J., "The Upanishads", in Approaches to the Asian Classics (1990)
Anquetil-Duperron, Oupnek'hat (1801–02) — the transmission document itself
Black, B., The Character of the Self in Ancient India (SUNY, 2007)

dating.display: c. 1200–1000 BCE (compilation; verses mostly drawn from the Ṛgveda)
dating.basis: Standard Vedic relative chronology: post-Ṛgvedic compilation, pre-Upaniṣadic.

summary: Nearly all its 1,875 verses come from the Ṛgveda — what is new is the music: the sāman chants stretch, ornament, and interpolate syllables (stobhas) into elaborate melodic lines, preserved in oral notation and later hand-sign systems. It is the liturgical soundtrack of the soma sacrifice.
whyItMatters: The oldest continuously transmitted music tradition on record. Indian musicological tradition (Nāradīyā Śikṣā onward) treats the sāman scale as ancestral to later Indian music theory — a lineage claim the sources discuss with appropriate care.
honestyNote: 'Indian classical music comes from Sāmaveda' is the popular compression; the documented statement is thinner — a continuous chant practice plus later theorists' own claims of descent. The chant survives in a handful of śākhā lineages (Jaiminīya in Kerala/Tamil Nadu among the most archaic).

sources.label: Staal, F., "The Fidelity of Oral Tradition and the Origins of Science" (1986)
Howard, W., Sāmavedic Chant (Yale, 1977)
Jamison, S. & Witzel, M., "Vedic Hinduism" (1992 survey) — corpus chronology

dating.display: c. 800–350 BCE (Baudhāyana oldest, then Āpastamba, Kātyāyana, Mānava)
dating.basis: Dated by language and position within the Kalpasūtra literature; no external anchors.

positions.holders: Standard histories of Indian mathematics (Plofker; Sen & Bag)
positions.evidence: Sūtra-period language; presupposed by later ritual literature.

positions.position: Considerably earlier core, contemporary with Brāhmaṇa ritual
positions.evidence: The altar constructions presuppose the geometry; a minority reading of the priority question.

summary: Manuals for building Vedic fire altars with cords and pegs: they state the diagonal rule ('the diagonal of a rectangle produces both areas...') — the 'Pythagorean' theorem in words, before or around Pythagoras — give √2 correct to five decimal places (1 + 1/3 + 1/(3·4) − 1/(3·4·34)), constructions for squaring shapes, and Pythagorean triples, all in the service of altar proportions.
whyItMatters: The earliest South Asian mathematical texts, and the standard exhibit for mathematics growing out of ritual requirements. The theorem statement is genuinely early and genuinely independent — and genuinely a statement, not a proof, which is the honest boundary of the claim.
honestyNote: No proofs are given; these are construction manuals. 'Baudhāyana discovered the Pythagorean theorem before Pythagoras' is defensible for the statement (and Babylonian tablets state cases earlier still); claims of 'proof' or of transmission to Greece are not supported.

throughLines.to: Geometry — early exact statement of the right-triangle rule and root approximations
chain: Baudhāyana Śulbasūtra 1.9–1.12: the diagonal rule and √2 approximation
Later Indian mathematics (Āryabhaṭa onward) builds on the computational tradition
Modern histories of mathematics credit the Śulbasūtras' priority for the explicit general statement in South Asia

sources.label: Sen, S.N. & Bag, A.K., The Śulbasūtras (Indian National Science Academy, 1983) — critical edition and translation
Plofker, K., Mathematics in India (2009), ch. 2
Sen, S.N. & Bag, A.K., The Śulbasūtras (1983)
Plofker, K., Mathematics in India (2009)
Seidenberg, A., "The Ritual Origin of Geometry", Archive for History of Exact Sciences 1 (1962)

dating.display: astronomical epoch c. 1400–1200 BCE; composition possibly c. 700–500 BCE
dating.basis: The text's stated solstice position (winter solstice at Śraviṣṭhā/Delphini) fits c. 1400–1200 BCE by precession; its language looks later — so epoch and composition may be centuries apart.

positions.position: The astronomical data preserve a genuine 2nd-millennium observation; the text as we have it is later
positions.holders: Standard history of the exact sciences (Pingree, Plofker)

positions.position: Composition itself is as old as the epoch
positions.evidence: Takes the solstice statement as contemporary observation; reported as a position.

summary: Lagadha's short verse manual (Ṛk and Yajus recensions) for scheduling Vedic sacrifice: a five-year yuga calendar of 62 synodic months with intercalation rules, day-length ratios, and nakṣatra positions — India's oldest surviving astronomical text.
whyItMatters: It shows quantitative, predictive astronomy embedded in ritual needs, and its solstice datum is a rare astronomical time-anchor inside the Vedic corpus — used carefully, it constrains chronology; used carelessly, it fuels date inflation in both directions.

sources.label: Kuppanna Sastry, T.S., Vedāṅga Jyotiṣa of Lagadha (INSA, 1985) — edition, translation, epoch analysis
Pingree, D., "The Mesopotamian Origin of Early Indian Mathematical Astronomy", JHA 4 (1973) — the skeptical-transmission reading
Plofker, K., Mathematics in India (2009), ch. 2

title.en: Early iron metallurgy in the Ganga plain

dating.display: c. 1800–1000 BCE at sites in the central Ganga plain and Vindhyas
dating.basis: Radiocarbon-dated iron-bearing levels at Malhar, Raja Nala-ka-Tila, Dadupur and related sites (Tewari 2003), corroborated by subsequent work.

summary: Smelted iron — furnaces, slag, artifacts — appears in the Ganga–Vindhya zone by the early 2nd millennium BCE, earlier than older textbooks assumed and plausibly an independent development rather than a diffusion from the west.
whyItMatters: Recalibrates the material backdrop of the Vedic period: kṛṣṇa ayas ('dark metal') in later Vedic texts now has a local, dated metallurgical context. Also a corrective in both directions — against 'iron came late and only from outside', and against inflating these dates further than the labs do.

locations.name: Malhar, Chandauli District, Uttar Pradesh, India
locations.note: approximate; one of several dated sites

sources.label: Tewari, R., "The origins of iron-working in India: new evidence from the Central Ganga Plain and the Eastern Vindhyas", Antiquity 77 (2003)
Chakrabarti, D.K., The Early Use of Iron in India (OUP, 1992)
Johansen, P., overviews of South Asian iron chronology in Journal of World Prehistory (2014)

title.en: Painted Grey Ware culture and Hastināpura

dating.display: c. 1200–600 BCE across the Ganga–Yamuna doab
dating.basis: Stratified sequences from B.B. Lal's Hastinapura excavation (1950–52) onward; widely replicated.

summary: A fine wheel-made grey pottery with black-painted patterns marks the Iron Age villages of the western Ganga plain — the region and rough period in which later Vedic texts situate the Kuru realm. Lal's Hastinapura dig famously found a flood-truncated PGW level, which he correlated with the Mahābhārata tradition of Hastināpura's flooding.
whyItMatters: PGW is the standard archaeological horizon put in conversation with the later Vedic world and epic geography — the strongest available case study in how (and how far) texts and strata can be linked.
honestyNote: Pottery is not a people and not a text: 'PGW = Vedic Kurus' is a correlation argued from geography and chronology, not an identification the sherds themselves can prove. The Mahābhārata flood correlation is evocative, unverifiable, and labeled as such by careful archaeologists.

locations.name: Hastinapura, Meerut District, Uttar Pradesh, India

sources.label: Lal, B.B., "Excavation at Hastināpura and other explorations", Ancient India 10–11 (1954–55)
Witzel, M., "Early Sanskritization: Origins and Development of the Kuru State", EJVS 1 (1995)
Singh, U., A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India (2008) — PGW synthesis

dating.display: codified in the Brāhmaṇa/Sūtra period (c. 900–500 BCE); still performed
dating.basis: Textual codification dated by the standard Vedic sequence; the living performances are directly documented.

summary: The most elaborate Vedic śrauta rite: twelve days of ritual centered on building a five-layer, thousand-brick fire altar in the shape of a bird — the rite whose geometry the Śulbasūtras exist to serve. In 1975 a full Agnicayana was performed by Nambudiri brahmins in Panjal, Kerala, and documented exhaustively by Frits Staal's team.
whyItMatters: A ritual system codified ~2,500+ years ago, still performable from living memory, is the atlas's strongest continuity datum — not inferred from artifacts but filmed. It anchors the claim that Vedic tradition is a living transmission, without needing any embellishment.
honestyNote: Continuity of ritual form does not mean frozen identity of meaning or of society around it; performances are now rare, and the 1975 event itself involved compromises (documented in Staal's volumes) that show tradition adapting to survive.

locations.name: Panjal, Thrissur District, Kerala, India — 1975 documented performance

throughLines.to: Living practice, continuously transmitted to the present
chain: Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa and śrauta sūtras codify the rite (c. 900–500 BCE)
Unbroken (if narrowing) Nambudiri and other śrauta lineages carry the recitation and procedure
1975 Panjal Agnicayana filmed and published in full (Staal, AGNI, 2 vols., 1983); later performances continue

sources.label: Staal, F., AGNI: The Vedic Ritual of the Fire Altar, 2 vols. (1983)
UNESCO ICH: Tradition of Vedic Chanting — the transmission context
Staal, F., AGNI: The Vedic Ritual of the Fire Altar (1983)
Eggeling, J. (tr.), Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa (SBE, 1882–1900)
Plofker, K., Mathematics in India (2009) — altar geometry context
